Insolvency and Bankruptcy Code 
 
Provisions 
Preamble  
‚ĚĖ‚Äč The IBC aims to consolidate and amend laws relating to insolvency and bankruptcy of companies, LLPs, individuals, and 
partnership firms. 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč Its objectives are: 
‚ěĘ‚Äč Time-bound insolvency resolution. 
‚ěĘ‚Äč Maximisation of asset value of the debtor. 
‚ěĘ‚Äč Promotion of entrepreneurship and availability of credit. 
‚ěĘ‚Äč Balancing interests of all stakeholders. 
‚ěĘ‚Äč Establishing an institutional framework (IBBI, IPs, IPAs, IUs, NCLT/NCLAT). 
‚ěĘ‚Äč Encouraging resolution over liquidation wherever possible.

Insolvency and Bankruptcy Code, 2016 (IBC 2016) 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč Section 3: Definitions 
‚ěĘ‚Äč Section 3(6): A claim means: 
‚Ė†‚Äč
Right to payment ‚Äď whether or not it is reduced to judgment, disputed, undisputed, secured, unsecured, 
fixed, contingent, matured, unmatured. 
‚Ė†‚Äč
Right to remedy for breach of contract ‚Äď if such breach gives rise to a right of payment. 
‚ěĘ‚Äč Section 3(7): Corporate Person includes: 
‚Ė†‚Äč
A company 
‚Ė†‚Äč
An LLP 
‚Ė†‚Äč
Any other incorporated entity 
‚Ė†‚Äč
But excludes financial services providers (except where notified) 
‚ěĘ‚Äč Section 3(8): Corporate Debtor ‚Äď corporate person who owes a debt to any person. 
‚ěĘ‚Äč Section 3(10): Creditor ‚Äď means any person to whom a debt is owed and includes 
‚Ė†‚Äč
financial creditors, 
‚Ė†‚Äč
operational creditors, 
‚Ė†‚Äč
secured creditors, 
‚Ė†‚Äč
unsecured creditors, 
‚Ė†‚Äč
decree-holders, etc. 
‚ěĘ‚Äč Section 3(11): Debt ‚Äď a liability or obligation in respect of a claim ‚ÄĒ financial or operational. 
‚ěĘ‚Äč Section 3(12) ‚Äď Default  [default is of a debt (which is a liability to pay)] 
‚Ė†‚Äč
Definition: non-payment of debt when whole or any part of the instalment becomes due and payable and 
is not paid by the debtor. 
‚Ė†‚Äč
Difference between ‚Äúdebt‚ÄĚ and ‚Äúdefault‚ÄĚ: 
‚óŹ‚Äč
Debt = liability in respect of a claim. 
‚óŹ‚Äč
Default = non-payment of that liability. 
5 
 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč Section 4: Application of the Code (threshold amounts) 
‚ěĘ‚Äč Applies to matters where the minimum amount of default is ‚āĻ1 crore 
‚Ė†‚Äč
This applies uniformly to Section 7, 8, 9, and 10 petitions. 
‚Ė†‚Äč
Does not apply to personal guarantor proceedings under Part III. 
‚ěĘ‚Äč Government notification power 
‚Ė†‚Äč
The Central Government may specify different thresholds for different classes of persons. 
‚Ė†‚Äč
E.g., real estate homebuyers were considered separately in certain policy discussions. 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč Section 5: Definitions  
‚ěĘ‚Äč Section 5(6) ‚Äď Existence of dispute 
‚Ė†‚Äč
Includes [not means, which was removed ‚Üí not exhaustive] suit or arbitration or any proceeding pending 
before an authority relating to: 
‚óŹ‚Äč
amount of debt, 
‚óŹ‚Äč
quality of goods, 
‚óŹ‚Äč
breach of contract, etc. 
‚Ė†‚Äč
Mobilox Doctrine: 
‚óŹ‚Äč
A dispute must be: 
‚óÜ‚Äč
Pre-existing (before Section 8 notice) 
‚óÜ‚Äč
Plausible 
‚óÜ‚Äč
Not feeble, spurious, illusory, or unsupported. 
‚Ė†‚Äč
NCLT is not a civil court; it does not examine merits. 
‚óŹ‚Äč
Only examines existence, not justification. 
‚ěĘ‚Äč Section 5(7): Financial Creditor ‚Äď means any person to whom a financial debt is owed and includes those to 
whom such debt has been legally assigned or transferred. 
‚ěĘ‚Äč Section 5(8): Financial Debt 
‚Ė†‚Äč
A debt along with interest, if any, disbursed against the consideration for the time value of money. 
‚Ė†‚Äč
Includes: 
‚óŹ‚Äč
Money borrowed 
‚óŹ‚Äč
Letters of credit 
‚óŹ‚Äč
Bonds, notes 
‚óŹ‚Äč
Finance leases treated as loans 
‚óŹ‚Äč
Receivables financing 
‚óŹ‚Äč
Derivative transactions 
‚óŹ‚Äč
Counter-indemnity obligations 
‚óŹ‚Äč
Guarantees for financial debts 
‚óŹ‚Äč
Any amount raised under note-purchase agreements 
‚ěĘ‚Äč Section 5(20): Operational Creditor ‚Äď a person to whom an operational debt is owed. 
‚ěĘ‚Äč Section 5(21): Operational Debt ‚Äď a claim in respect of [this phrase makes the provision more broadly 
applicable]: 
‚Ė†‚Äč
Provision of goods or services, including employment; or 
‚Ė†‚Äč
A debt arising under a statute (government dues, taxes, duties); 
‚Ė†‚Äč
Payable to central or state government or local authority. 
‚ěĘ‚Äč Section 5(6): Dispute ‚Äď includes suit, arbitration, or any proceeding before any authority. 
‚ěĘ‚Äč Section 5(12): Insolvency Commencement Date ‚Üí Date of admission of application under Section 7, 9, or 10. 
‚ěĘ‚Äč Section 5(13): Interim Resolution Professional ‚Äď appointed under Section 16. 
‚ěĘ‚Äč Section 5(26): Resolution Plan ‚Äď Plan proposed by resolution applicant for insolvency resolution. 
‚ěĘ‚Äč Section 5(25): Resolution Applicant ‚Äď Person submitting resolution plan. 
‚Ė†‚Äč
Contents of the plan ‚Äď reg 37 
‚ěĘ‚Äč Section 5(24): Related Party ‚Äď includes: 
‚Ė†‚Äč
Promoters, directors, KMP 
6 
 
‚Ė†‚Äč
Relatives 
‚Ė†‚Äč
Bodies corporate interconnected through shareholding 
‚Ė†‚Äč
Entities with participation in policy decisions 
‚ěĘ‚Äč Section 5(27): Resolution Professional ‚Äď central to all CIRP procedures. 
 
Initiation of CIRP (Core Provisions) 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč Section 6 ‚Äď Persons who may initiate CIRP 
‚ěĘ‚Äč Where any corporate debtor commits a default, a CIRP may be initiated by: 
‚Ė†‚Äč
A financial creditor (Section 7) 
‚Ė†‚Äč
An operational creditor (Section 9) 
‚Ė†‚Äč
The corporate debtor itself (Section 10) 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč Section 7 ‚Äď CIRP by financial creditor 
‚ěĘ‚Äč FC or group of FC may file on occurrence of default. 
‚ěĘ‚Äč Creditors must provide: Evidence of default (information utility records, loan documents, bank statements). 
‚ěĘ‚Äč Proposed IRP (optional). AA (NCLT) must admit if default is established and the application is complete. 
‚ěĘ‚Äč No requirement to issue notice prior to filing (unlike Section 8 for operational creditors). 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč Section 8 ‚Äď Insolvency notice by operational creditor 
‚ěĘ‚Äč OC must first deliver a demand notice to the corporate debtor. 
‚ěĘ‚Äč Demand notice may be either: 
‚Ė†‚Äč
Notice of unpaid operational debt; or 
‚Ė†‚Äč
Copy of invoice demanding payment. 
‚ěĘ‚Äč Corporate debtor has 10 days to respond by: 
‚Ė†‚Äč
Proving pre-existing dispute (Section 5(6)), or 
‚Ė†‚Äč
Demonstrating payment, set-off, or counter-claims. 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč Section 9 ‚Äď CIRP by operational creditor 
‚ěĘ‚Äč Filed after expiry of 10 days from Section 8 demand notice. 
‚ěĘ‚Äč OC must show: 
‚Ė†‚Äč
Debt, 
‚Ė†‚Äč
Default, 
‚Ė†‚Äč
No pre-existing dispute. 
‚ěĘ‚Äč 9(3) has a list of documents to be submitted by the OC. 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč Section 10 ‚Äď CIRP by corporate applicant 
‚ěĘ‚Äč Who can apply: CD itself 
‚Ė†‚Äč
Through shareholders 
‚Ė†‚Äč
Through directors 
‚Ė†‚Äč
Through employees (rare) 
‚Ė†‚Äč
Through persons authorised by board resolution. 
‚ěĘ‚Äč Requirements: 
‚Ė†‚Äč
Special resolution or 3/4 partner approval. 
‚Ė†‚Äč
Books of account, financial statements. 
‚Ė†‚Äč
Default must still be shown. 
‚ěĘ‚Äč Policy intention: 
‚Ė†‚Äč
Allows early insolvency, avoids value destruction. 
‚Ė†‚Äč
However, it is frequently misused for ‚Äúforum shopping‚ÄĚ, leading to amendments and stricter scrutiny. 
 
CIRP Management & Process 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč Section 12 ‚Äď Time limit for completion of CIRP 
‚ěĘ‚Äč Core Rule 
‚Ė†‚Äč
CIRP must be completed within 180 days from the date of admission. 
7 
 
‚Ė†‚Äč
One extension up to 90 days permitted, if CoC approves by 66% voting, and AA is satisfied. 
‚ěĘ‚Äč Outer Limit ‚Äď 365 days total (including litigation period) as per 2019 amendment 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč Section 13 ‚Äď Declaration of moratorium 
‚ěĘ‚Äč NCLT, upon admitting CIRP application: 
‚Ė†‚Äč
Declares moratorium under Section 14. 
‚Ė†‚Äč
Appoints IRP. 
‚Ė†‚Äč
Causes public announcement (Section 15). 
‚Ė†‚Äč
Calls for submission of claims. 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč Section 14 ‚Äď Moratorium 
‚ěĘ‚Äč Moratorium Applies To: 
‚Ė†‚Äč
No suits or proceedings against corporate debtors. 
‚Ė†‚Äč
No execution of judgments, decrees, orders. 
‚Ė†‚Äč
No transfer/encumbrance of assets. 
‚Ė†‚Äč
No foreclosure or recovery under SARFAESI. 
‚Ė†‚Äč
Supply of essential goods or services cannot be terminated. 
‚ěĘ‚Äč Moratorium Does NOT apply to: 
‚Ė†‚Äč
Personal guarantors ‚Äď clarified by Dilip Jiwrajka. 
‚Ė†‚Äč
Criminal proceedings. 
‚Ė†‚Äč
Regulatory proceedings not seeking money recovery (SEBI, CCI etc.). 
‚Ė†‚Äč
Termination of contract with ipso facto clauses ‚Üí restricted by Gujarat Urja (2021) which held power 
purchase agreements cannot be terminated if essential to CIRP. 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč Section 15 [+ Reg 6]‚Äď Public Announcements ‚Äď IRP must publish: 
‚ěĘ‚Äč Name and details of debtor 
‚ěĘ‚Äč Last date for submission of claims 
‚ěĘ‚Äč Details of IRP 
‚ěĘ‚Äč Penalties for false claims 
‚ěĘ‚Äč Reg 6A ‚Äď must also notify all creditors from books of account. 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč Section 16 ‚Äď Appointment of IRP 
‚ěĘ‚Äč Appointed by NCLT for 30 days. 
‚ěĘ‚Äč Can be proposed by FC (Section 7) or operational creditor. 
‚ěĘ‚Äč IRP takes over management (Section 17). 
‚ěĘ‚Äč Replacement possible under Section 22. 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč Section 17 ‚Äď Management of Affairs of CD 
‚ěĘ‚Äč Upon CIRP admission: 
‚Ė†‚Äč
Management vests in IRP. 
‚Ė†‚Äč
Board of directors suspended. 
‚Ė†‚Äč
Officers/employees must report to IRP. 
‚Ė†‚Äč
Banks must freeze existing operations and give control to IRP. 
‚ěĘ‚Äč This is the creditor-in-control model (Swiss Ribbons). 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč Section 18 ‚Äď Duties of IRP 
‚ěĘ‚Äč Collect financial information 
‚ěĘ‚Äč Constitute CoC 
‚ěĘ‚Äč Verify claims [reg 13] 
8 
 
‚ěĘ‚Äč
 
‚ěĘ‚Äč Take control and custody of assets 
‚ěĘ‚Äč Protect and preserve the value of the CD 
‚ěĘ‚Äč Access records from information utilities, ROC, etc. 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč Section 19 ‚Äď Personnel to Extend Corporation  
‚ěĘ‚Äč Directors, officers, auditors must cooperate with IRP/RP. 
‚ěĘ‚Äč Non-cooperation ‚Üí NCLT may order imprisonment/fines. 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč Section 20 ‚Äď Management of Operations as Going Concern ‚Äď IRP/RP must: 
‚ěĘ‚Äč Ensure continuation of business. 
‚ěĘ‚Äč Raise interim finance (with CoC approval under Section 28). 
‚ěĘ‚Äč Act to protect the value of assets. 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč Section 21 ‚Äď Committee of Creditors 
‚ěĘ‚Äč Constitution w/i 2 days of verification of claims [Reg 40A model timelines] 
‚ěĘ‚Äč Composition 
‚Ė†‚Äč
Only FCs. 
‚Ė†‚Äč
Related-party FCs are excluded. 
‚Ė†‚Äč
If no FCs, then operational creditors (OCs) as per regulation. 
‚ěĘ‚Äč Voting 
‚Ė†‚Äč
Voting share is proportional to debt. 
‚Ė†‚Äč
66% for major decisions (appointment, extension, approval of plans). 
‚Ė†‚Äč
51% for routine matters. 
‚ěĘ‚Äč Powers ‚Äď CoC controls entire resolution process, including: 
‚Ė†‚Äč
Appointment of RP (Section 22) 
‚Ė†‚Äč
Replacement (Section 27) 
‚Ė†‚Äč
Approving resolution plan (Section 30) 
‚Ė†‚Äč
Approving actions under Section 28 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč Section 22‚Äď27 ‚Äď Appointment/replacement of Resolution Professional 
‚ěĘ‚Äč Section 22 ‚Äď Appointment of RP 
‚Ė†‚Äč
CoC (66%) confirms IRP as RP or replaces him. 
‚Ė†‚Äč
New RP appointed by NCLT. 
‚ěĘ‚Äč Section 23 ‚Äď RP to Conduct Entire CIRP 
‚Ė†‚Äč
Continues until completion. 
‚Ė†‚Äč
RP submits a resolution plan to NCLT. 
9 
 
‚ěĘ‚Äč Section 24 ‚Äď Meetings of CoC 
‚Ė†‚Äč
RP chairs meetings. 
‚Ė†‚Äč
OCs may attend but have no voting rights. 
‚óŹ‚Äč
The amount of their aggregate dues is not less than ten percent. 
‚Ė†‚Äč
 
‚ěĘ‚Äč Section 25 ‚Äď Duties of RP ‚Äď Major duties: 
‚Ė†‚Äč
Preserve assets 
‚Ė†‚Äč
Invite resolution plans 
‚Ė†‚Äč
Maintain updated records 
‚Ė†‚Äč
Run business as going concern 
‚ěĘ‚Äč Section 26 ‚Äď Avoidance Transactions ‚Äď filing of PUFE/avoidance applications does not affect the resolution plan 
timeline. 
‚ěĘ‚Äč Section 27 ‚Äď Replacement of RP 
‚Ė†‚Äč
CoC may replace RP with 66% vote. 
‚Ė†‚Äč
NCLT must appoint the new RP. 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč Section 28 ‚Äď Actions requiring CoC approval ‚Äď RP cannot take the following without 66% CoC vote: 
‚ěĘ‚Äč Raising interim finance beyond threshold 
‚ěĘ‚Äč Creating security interest 
‚ěĘ‚Äč Change capital structure 
‚ěĘ‚Äč Undertake related-party transactions 
‚ěĘ‚Äč Amend constitutional documents 
‚ěĘ‚Äč Disposal of assets not in ordinary course 
‚ěĘ‚Äč Undertaking major financial decisions 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč Section 29 ‚Äď Information memorandum ‚Äď prepared by RP, includes: 
‚ěĘ‚Äč Financial statements 
‚ěĘ‚Äč List of creditors 
‚ěĘ‚Äč Assets and liabilities 
‚ěĘ‚Äč Litigation status 
‚ěĘ‚Äč Guarantees 
‚ěĘ‚Äč Material contracts 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč Section 60 ‚Äď AA 
‚ěĘ‚Äč Section 60(1) ‚Äď NCLT is the Adjudicating Authority for: 
‚Ė†‚Äč
Corporate persons 
‚Ė†‚Äč
Corporate debtors 
‚Ė†‚Äč
Personal guarantors (post-2019 amendment) 
‚ěĘ‚Äč Section 60(2) ‚Äď when CIRP is pending before NCLT, all insolvency proceedings relating to personal guarantors 
must be filed before the same NCLT. 
10 
 
‚ěĘ‚Äč Section 60(3) ‚ÄĒ Transfer of Pending Guarantor Proceedings ‚Äď Any insolvency/bankruptcy proceeding already 
pending in any court/DRT against the personal guarantor must be transferred to the NCLT where the corporate 
debtor‚Äôs CIRP is pending. 
‚ěĘ‚Äč Section 60(4) ‚ÄĒ NCLT‚Äôs Powers Under the Companies Act 2013 
‚Ė†‚Äč
For IBC purposes, the NCLT has all powers of: 
‚óŹ‚Äč
National Company Law Tribunal 
‚óŹ‚Äč
Debt Recovery Tribunal (DRT) 
‚óŹ‚Äč
as if those proceedings were under their respective laws. 
‚ěĘ‚Äč Section 60(5) ‚Äď Residuary Jurisdiction ‚Äď NCLT has jurisdiction over: 
‚Ė†‚Äč
Any question of law or fact relating to IBC. 
‚Ė†‚Äč
Avoidance transactions. 
‚Ė†‚Äč
Contract termination issues (see Gujarat Urja). 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč Section 61 ‚Äď Appeals 
‚ěĘ‚Äč Section 61(1) ‚Äď Appeal from NCLT to NCLAT 
‚ěĘ‚Äč Any person aggrieved by an order of the Adjudicating Authority (NCLT) under S 7-10, 31 (approval of plan), 33 
(liquidation order) may prefer an appeal to NCLAT. 
‚ěĘ‚Äč Time limit: 30 days, with a possible extension of 15 days for sufficient cause. (No further extension permitted.) 
‚ěĘ‚Äč Section 61(2) ‚Äď Grounds of Appeal 
‚Ė†‚Äč
Appeal against an order approving a resolution plan under Section 31 or passing liquidation under 
Section 33 may be filed only on the following enumerated grounds: 
‚Ė†‚Äč
Contravention of provisions of law 
‚Ė†‚Äč
Material irregularity in exercise of powers by the resolution professional 
‚Ė†‚Äč
Non-compliance with Section 30(2) ‚Äď the resolution plan does not meet the statutory requirements listed 
in Section 30(2), including: 
‚óŹ‚Äč
payment of CIRP costs, 
‚óŹ‚Äč
minimum payments to operational creditors, 
‚óŹ‚Äč
mechanism for implementation, 
‚óŹ‚Äč
Does not comply with other mandatory elements. 
‚Ė†‚Äč
Improper approval by CoC ‚Äď the resolution plan has not been approved by the Committee of Creditors 
in the manner required by the Code. 
‚Ė†‚Äč
Material irregularity in relation to liquidation order ‚Äď if the liquidation order suffers from material 
irregularities. 
‚ěĘ‚Äč Section 61(3) ‚Äď Appeal from NCLAT to Supreme Court 
‚Ė†‚Äč
Any person aggrieved by an NCLAT order may appeal to the Supreme Court on a question of law. 
‚Ė†‚Äč
Time limit: 45 days, extendable by 15 days for sufficient cause. 
‚ěĘ‚Äč Section 61(4) Filing of appeal does not by itself grant a stay; stay requires an order. 
‚ěĘ‚Äč Section 61(5) ‚Äď Provides the mechanical and procedural aspects for hearing the appeal (service, records, etc.). 
 
Resolution Plan: Key Provisions  
‚ĚĖ‚Äč Section 30 ‚Äď Submission of resolution plan 
‚ěĘ‚Äč Who may submit a resolution plan (any eligible applicant; subject to Section 29A). 
‚ěĘ‚Äč Role of the RP to: 
‚Ė†‚Äč
Invite plans. 
‚Ė†‚Äč
Check each plan for Section 30(2) compliance. 
‚ěĘ‚Äč Mandatory contents of a resolution plan: [reg 38] 
‚Ė†‚Äč
Priority 
‚óŹ‚Äč
CIRP cost payment in full. [reg 31] 
‚óŹ‚Äč
Minimum payments to operational creditors. [formula] 
‚óŹ‚Äč
FC ‚Äď as per CoC approved plan ‚Äď may take a haircut ‚Äď priority within is decided by CoC. 
11 
 
‚óŹ‚Äč
Dissenting FC ‚Äď min payment and payment in priority ‚Äď  not less than the value they could have 
been paid in liquidation (53). 
‚Ė†‚Äč
Management and supervision of implementation. 
‚óŹ‚Äč
 
‚Ė†‚Äč
Statement on how the plan deals with creditors. 
‚ěĘ‚Äč Presentation of Resolution Plan to CoC  ‚Äď manner of submission [reg 39] 
‚ěĘ‚Äč Voting requirement under Section 30(4): 
‚Ė†‚Äč
CoC must approve the plan with 66% voting share. 
‚Ė†‚Äč
Feasibility and viability criteria for CoC evaluation. 
‚Ė†‚Äč
Manner of voting simultaneous on all plans ‚Äď reg 39(3A)/(3B) 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč
 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč Section 31 ‚Äď Approval of resolution plan 
‚ěĘ‚Äč After CoC approval, the plan must be placed before the NCLT, which must ensure compliance with Section 30(2) 
and other laws. 
‚ěĘ‚Äč Once approved by NCLT, the plan becomes binding on: 
‚Ė†‚Äč
Corporate debtor 
‚Ė†‚Äč
Employees 
‚Ė†‚Äč
Members 
‚Ė†‚Äč
Creditors (financial + operational) 
‚Ė†‚Äč
Government authorities 
‚Ė†‚Äč
Guarantors 
‚ěĘ‚Äč Gives statutory recognition to the ‚Äúclean slate‚ÄĚ principle. 
‚ěĘ‚Äč Bars any claims not included in the approved plan (Ghanashyam Mishra). 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč Section 32 ‚Äď Immunity (post-approval; relevant for ArcelorMittal line) 
12 
 
‚ěĘ‚Äč Provided early judicial basis for protecting the corporate debtor from past offences once a new management took 
over. 
‚ěĘ‚Äč Treated the approved resolution plan as giving a fresh start.
